title: Intangible Capital in Factor Models
abstract: The transition from a traditional manufacturing-based economy to a knowledge- and service-based economy over recent decades resulted in a considerable rise in intangible capital, most of which is not reported on companies’ balance sheets. As a result, balance sheet-based valuation ratios, investment measures, and other firm characteristics that do not incorporate off-balance sheet (OBS) intangible capital suffer from significant measurement error problems. We incorporate a new measure of OBS intangible capital into firm characteristics, such as book to market, investment, and profitability, to address these measurement errors. These OBS intangible adjustments improve the performance of the Fama–French three- and five-factor models and theq-factor model, especially during recent decades. We further find that the value factor is no longer redundant in these empirical factor models.

author.Huseyin Gulen: Mitchell E. Daniels, Jr. School of Business, Purdue University, West Lafayette, Indiana 47097
author.Dongmei Li: Darla Moore School of Business, University of South Carolina, Columbia, South Carolina 29208
author.Ryan H. Peters: Freeman School of Business, Tulane University, New Orleans, Louisiana 70118
author.Morad Zekhnini: Broad College of Business, Michigan State University, East Lansing, Michigan 48824

article.1. Introduction: The transition from a traditional manufacturing-based economy to a knowledge- and service-based economy over recent decades has been marked by the dramatically rising importance of intangible investments in research and development (R&D) and organizational capital. For example,Corrado and Hulten (2010)estimate that 34% of firms’ total capital was intangible in the early 2000s. Similarly,Ewens et al. (2024)show that the number has climbed steadily in recent years (to around 50%).1Given the increasing weight of intangible assets in firms’ total capital, failure to incorporate intangible capital/investments properly in measures of corporate investment and valuation ratios (such as the book-to-market ratio (B/M) and profitability measures) introduces significant measurement error in such metrics and can correspondingly distort investors’ investment decisions. For example, categorizing a value/growth firm by the traditional B/M that ignores OBS intangibles can be misleading (see, e.g.,Lev and Srivastava 2022). Similarly, operating profitability (OP) and return on equity (ROE), two popular measures of profitability, can provide a distorted and short-sighted picture of a firm’s financial health and operational efficiency if the firm invests heavily in R&D and SG&A. Such investments immediately reduce operating profits and earnings under current U.S. accounting rules, but they are crucial to a firm’s long-term competitive advantage and success through their impact on innovation and human and brand capital (see, e.g.,Lev and Sougiannis 1996).2 These firm characteristics are not only important in predicting stock returns in the cross-section but also serve as the primary building blocks of popular empirical factor models. For example, theFama and French (1993)three-factor model (henceforth, FF3f), theFama and French (2015)five-factor model (henceforth, FF5f), and theHou et al. (2015)q-factor model (henceforth, HXZ) rely on these firm-level characteristics (e.g., the book-to-market ratio, investment rate, and profitability measures) in constructing corresponding factors.3Because these firm-level characteristics all but ignore intangible investment, the resulting factors can also be subject to substantial measurement error. In this paper, we investigate the implications of neglecting intangible investments on both cross-sectional stock return predictability and the performance of popular factor models. To illustrate the measurement error introduced by failing to incorporate OBS intangibles in price-scaled variables, we plot the traditional book-to-market ratio over time in panel (a) ofFigure 1. The downward trend in B/M mirrors the upward trend in off-balance sheet intangible capital. To assess the extent to which this trend is driven by the omission of OBS intangible capital in the B/M calculation, in panel (b) ofFigure 1, we plot an alternative measure of a price-scaled variable that incorporates OBS intangible capital properly: free cash flow to price ratio. Because free cash flow captures the cash that can be disbursed to investors, it does not suffer from the same mismeasurement problems associated with book values of assets/equity. In other words, any artificial change because of accounting standards will not affect free cash flows as long as it does not affect the fundamental operation of the firm. As is evident inFigure 1, the downward trend in book values (relative to market values) is not associated with a similar trend in free cash flows. The fact that cash flows have remained stable while book equity (BE) declined suggests that some productive capital is not reflected in the GAAP measures. This illustrates the need to properly account for OBS intangibles in firm valuation ratios and other firm characteristics that are used in forming factor-mimicking portfolios in factor models. To provide a theoretical motivation for incorporating intangible capital in empirical factor models, we extend the motivating framework of the dividend discount model proposed byFama and French (2006,2015) as well as theq-theory model ofHou et al. (2015). In our theoretical framework (seeSection 2andAppendix A), we demonstrate the need for separately considering on- and off-balance sheet assets as well as tangible and intangible investments.4We extend the framework ofFama and French (2006,2015) to highlight the differences between balance sheet assets, which are dominated by tangible capital, and off-balance sheet intangible assets. To the extent that the investment intensities associated with these two types of assets differ significantly, combining on- and off-balance sheet book equity together in a total measure may obscure the significant heterogeneity between balance sheet B/M and off-balance sheet B/M (henceforth BOBS/M) in predicting stock returns. In fact, recent studies (see, e.g.,Eisfeldt and Papanikolaou 2013,Ewens et al. 2024) show that investment rates of intangible assets indeed exceed those of tangible assets significantly, especially over recent decades. A similar adjustment to theq-theory model ofHou et al. (2015)also results in distinct investment-return relationships for tangible and intangible assets. Implicitly, HXZ only considers tangible investment in the theoretical framework and the correspondingq-factor model. By explicitly modeling tangible and intangible investments in their framework, we illustrate that the relationship between these two types of investments and expected returns can be substantially different and that this difference depends on the relative importance of intangible capital and its adjustment costs. Thus, to better understand the independent contribution that intangibles play in popular factor models, we augment FF3f, FF5f, and HXZ with intangibles-based factors.5For example, to address the measurement error in the value factor (HML), we first construct two separate BE measures: (i) original BE, which captures balance sheet items, and (ii) a BE measure based on off-balance sheet intangible assets only (BEOBS). We then construct two value factors (HML and HMLOBS) based on the corresponding book-to-market equity ratios (B/M and BOBS/M), respectively.6Using a similar procedure, we construct two measures of investment: the growth in property, plant, and equipment (PPE) (PPEG) and the growth in off-balance sheet intangibles (OBSG). Finally, we adjust the operating profitability and the return on equity measures to reflect the hypothetical capitalization of intangible investment (OPtotaland ROEtotal). We then follow the procedures ofFama and French (2015)andHou et al. (2015)to build corresponding investment factors (CMAPPE, CMAOBS, qINVPPE, qINVOBS) and profitability factors (RMWtotaland qROEtotal). To test the empirical implications of these adjustments, we first compare the return predictability of the underlying characteristics (traditional versus intangible-adjusted versions) using portfolio sorts. We find that intangible-adjusted characteristics provide significantly stronger return predictability in general. For example, in characteristic-sorted decile portfolios, we show that both versions of B/M, investment, and profitability result in sizeable return spreads but often differ significantly in magnitude across the two methods. Specifically, over the sample period from July 1998 to June 2022, sorts based on B/M, PPEG, ROE, and OP yield value-weighted (equal-weighted) monthly return spreads of −0.02%, −0.10%, 0.50%, and 0.44%, respectively (0.86%, −0.82%, 0.90%, and 0.37%, respectively). Corresponding value-weighted (equal-weighted) returns for BOBS/M, OBSG, ROEtotal, and OPtotalare 0.46%, −0.20%, 0.73%, and 0.99%, respectively (1.29%, −0.67%, 1.08%, and 0.64%, respectively). Thus, the portfolio sorts point to the importance of incorporating OBS intangibles in measuring these key characteristics. Consistent with the evidence presented, we find that incorporating OBS intangibles into the construction of the value and profitability factors in the FF3f, FF5f, and HXZ factor models results in sizeable improvements in these individual factors over recent decades, during which time intangibles became increasingly important. We show that the deterioration of the value premium over the past two decades (see, e.g.,Fama and French 2021,Lev and Srivastava 2022) can be at least partially attributed to the increase in the share of intangibles in total capital and the failure of classical measures of the book-to-market ratio to incorporate OBS intangible assets. For example, from July 1998 to June 2022, the average monthly value premium associated with HML is merely seven basis points (bps;t-statistic = 0.34) with a Sharpe ratio (SR) of 0.02. In contrast, the average value premium associated with HMLOBSis 49 bps (t-statistic = 3.42, SR = 0.20). Similarly, incorporating intangibles into the FF5f profitability factor (RMW) improves its premium and Sharpe ratio significantly. For example, the average premiums associated with RMW and RMWtotalin the same period are 39 bps (t-statistic = 2.14, SR = 0.13) and 45 bps (t-statistic = 3.19, SR = 0.19), respectively. For the HXZ profitability factor (qROE), intangible adjustments do not result in similar improvements. However, this is not surprising given the limitation of sparse coverage of quarterly data for R&D and SG&A.7In addition, the intangible-based investment factors exceed the PPE-based investment factors in both average premium and Sharpe ratio. We then compare intangible-adjusted factor models with the versions that do not include intangibles-related factors using the Sharpe ratio test proposed inBarillas et al. (2020). This test considers the number of factors in the models for a fair comparison and the sample size used to address small sample biases. Therefore, models ranked high by this test also perform better in pricing concrete sets of test assets. We find that incorporating intangibles often improves the maximum Sharpe ratio of these benchmark models substantially and significantly over recent decades.8For example, augmenting FF3f with HMLOBSalmost doubles the model’s maximum Sharpe ratio from 0.13 to 0.25 with a substantial optimal weight on HMLOBSof 132% (the weights on the market factor, the size factor, and HML are only 33%, −35%, and −30% in the augmented model, respectively). We also find intangible adjustments to the PPE-based FF5f result in a significant improvement over recent decades. The adjustments increase the maximum SR from 0.27 to 0.40, and the difference is statistically significant. Although intangible adjustments to the investment and ROE factors in HXZ increase the model’s maximum SR by 10%, the improvement is insignificant. Given the similarity between FF5f and HXZ and the important role of HMLOBSobserved in the adjusted FF5f, we also examine its role in the HXZ model by adding HML and HML to the intangible-adjusted HXZ. The results show that adding these value factors in addition to the intangible adjustment to the investment and ROE factors improves the maximum Sharpe ratio significantly from 0.27 for HXZ to 0.41 with a substantial weight on both HML (−0.16) and HMLOBS(0.47). Overall, these results suggest that HML is no longer redundant in the FF5f and HXZ models once OBS intangibles are included in factor construction. Similarly, we find that the empirical performance of FF3f has been significantly understated in the literature because of the failure to properly account for intangibles. Interestingly, when we compare the empirical performance of FF5f and HXZ (either the unadjusted or intangible-adjusted versions) over recent decades, we do not find a significant difference between them. However, the intangible-adjusted FF5f significantly outperforms the unadjusted HXZ in terms of Sharpe ratio (0.40 versus 0.27). We also note that the lack of accurate measures of quarterly R&D and SG&A (as mentioned) may introduce significant noise to these comparisons.9 Consistent with the SR tests, intangible-adjusted factor models outperform the FF5f and HXZ models in explaining existing anomalies as well. For example, intangible adjustments to FF5f reduce the average (median) magnitude of the monthly alphas for the hedge portfolios of 81 anomalies by 14% (17%). The contrast in the percentage of anomalies that cannot be fully explained by a model is also big. We find that FF5f cannot explain 72% of these 81 anomalies at the 1% level. In contrast, intangible-adjusted FF5f cannot explain 57% of these anomalies. Similarly, the HXZ model cannot explain 68% of these anomalies, whereas the intangible-adjusted HXZ model cannot explain 60% of them. Our paper contributes to the literature that examines the asset pricing implications of intangible investment/assets, such as R&D investment, SG&A, advertising, patents, and trademarks.10There is also a growing literature that studies the effect of incorporating intangible assets into the value factoralone. For example, several recent and contemporaneous papers (see, e.g.,Park 2019,Amenc et al. 2020,Arnott et al. 2021,Eisfeldt et al. 2022) construct a revised HML factor by adding OBS intangibles to book equity, which is equivalent to our HMLtotal. We differ from these papers in several important ways. First, we show how incorporating intangibles systematically into prominent factor models improves these models significantly by considering the effect of intangibles on the value, investment, and profitability factors simultaneously. Second, we show both theoretically and empirically that it is important to treat the tangible and intangible investment/capital separately in making these intangible adjustments.11Third, we are the first to fully address the look-ahead bias in incorporating intangibles into the factors. Existing studies rely on intangible accumulation parameters estimated from the same/overlapping sample period as that used in constructing corresponding factors, and they are, therefore, subject to some look-ahead bias. In contrast, we construct the factors and conduct all the tests out of sample, eliminating the potential for this bias to affect our results.
article.2. Conceptual Frameworks with Intangible: To provide a theoretical motivation for incorporating intangibles to FF3f, FF5f, and HXZ, we extend the theoretical frameworks inFama and French (2015)andHou et al. (2015).Fama and French (2006,2015) use the dividend discount model of an infinitely lived firm in steady state to demonstrate the relationship between discount rates and profitability, investment, and book-to-market ratio. In contrast,Hou et al. (2015)motivate theirq-factor model with a two-periodq-theory model, wherein a firm chooses optimal investment. Both models predict a positive relation of expected returns to profitability and a negative relation to investment. The model ofFama and French (2015)additionally predicts a positive relation between expected returns and book to market. Neither model (explicitly) considers the implications of intangibles on the return predictability of these key characteristics. The literature has shown that the two types of investment differ in many aspects, such as reversibility, adjustment costs, skewness of future cash flow, etc.12Therefore, it is important to treat them separately in valuation ratios. As illustrated in our theoretical frameworks (seeAppendix A), the relation between expected returns and traditional B/M is clearly different from that between expected returns and BOBS/M because of differences in the two types of investment intensities. Aggregating to a single measure of B/M, as is the case in the literature, imposes a strong and unrealistic restriction that these two relationships are identical. Therefore, it is important to create two separate HML factors to capture the unique effect of intangibles on expected returns. Similarly, it is important to treat the two types of investment separately as detailed in our model inAppendix A. To further illustrate the importance of incorporating intangibles, we highlight the measurement errors in the investment and profitability measures associated with fully expensing of intangible investments. 2.1. Tangibility and Firm Characteristics BothFama and French (2015)andHou et al. (2015)use proxies for investment that ignore intangible assets not reported on the balance sheet.13However, R&D typically generates valuable intangible knowledge assets (such as patents and trademarks) that are not reflected on the balance sheet under current GAAP. Similarly, parts of SG&A expenses build intangible organizational capital, and they ought to be considered as a form of investment and capitalized as well (see, e.g.,Eisfeldt and Papanikolaou 2013). Along these lines, a more comprehensive measure of investment should include intangible investments in addition to capital expenditures. Finally, the various profitability measures used in factor construction do not address the implication of fully expensing intangible investments (R&D and SG&A). For example, the profitability factor (RMW) in FF5f is based on operating profitability (seeFama and French 2015) defined as sales minus cost of goods sold, interest expense, and R&D and SG&A expenses divided by traditional measure of book equity.14Conceptually, this measure captures a firm’s capability of converting inputs to outputs, where book equity is the proxy for inputs and operating profits is the proxy for outputs. Off-balance sheet intangible assets are important production inputs, especially for knowledge- and service-oriented industries, and therefore, they should be incorporated. Furthermore, the output may be underestimated under GAAP because R&D and SG&A are fully expensed. Correspondingly, the traditional measure of operating profitability may be misleading because it can overestimate/underestimate a firm’s true ability of converting relevant inputs to outputs. This mismeasurement of profitability, therefore, impacts the economic interpretation of the profitability premium. A similar argument applies to the profitability factor qROE in HXZ, which is based on ROE (the ratio of net income to book equity). Net income excludes intangible investments, and book equity does not account for the relevant off-balance sheet intangibles. In what follows, we discuss the proposed factor models that include additional value and investment factors that are based on OBS intangible capital and an adjustment to the profitability factor that better accounts for intangible capital. 2.2. Fama–French Model Extensions Fama and French (2006)use a dividend discount model to derive a relationship between returns, book-to-market, profitability, and investment. In their framework, they consider firm capital as a monolith and therefore, consider only one type of capital in their derivation. We extend their model (seeAppendix A) to break the value of a firm into two types of capital: tangible and intangible. As a result, our return equation includes tangible and intangible analogues of book to market, profitability, and investment. Given that we cannot construct valid proxies that separate the profitability of tangible and intangible capital, we propose that the empirical model should use a combined profitability measure. When considering the Fama–French factor models, we thus propose additional factors based on BOBS/M and OBSG. Our analogues to the FF3f and FF5f models are, therefore, given by 2.3. Hou et al. (2015) Model Extension Hou et al. (2015)model the optimal investment decision of a firm in a dynamic setting to derive a relationship between returns, investment, and profitability. The intuition behind the model is that given the same level of profitability, a firm invests more only if the discount rate (and hence, expected return) is low. Similarly, a higher profitability must be associated with higher expected return to justify a constant level of investment. InAppendix A, we formally extend their model, where we decompose the decisions of the firm into those related to tangible investment and those related to intangible investment. Based on the prior evidence that the parameters governing investments in intangible capital differ from those of tangible investment, the two rates of investment could be related to returns differently. As an empirical analogue to the factor model, we propose adding a factor related to intangible investment and adjusting the profitability factor to consider intangibles. The resulting factor model is given by Although the extendedq-factor framework does not indicate a role for B/M in return prediction, establishing such a link remains an empirical question. In order to compare the extended theoretical frameworks, we also augment theq-factors with HML and HMLOBSto investigate if value factors remain redundant in theq-framework. The model we use is given by
article.3. Estimation and Summary Statistics of: We estimate off-balance sheet intangible capital following the method inEwens et al. (2024). One advantage of this approach is that it better captures the heterogeneity in intangible capital accumulation across industries relative to other commonly used measures of intangible capital (e.g.,Peters and Taylor 2017). This heterogeneity induces significant variation in the estimated stock of intangible capital across industries. Specifically, we first estimate the intangible capital accumulation parameters (i.e.,𝛿𝐺δG, the depreciation rate for knowledge capitalG, and𝛾𝑆γS, the capitalization rate for SG&A expenses) based on market price of intangible assets derived from market trading price for public firms from 1974 to 1997 (as detailed later). We then compute knowledge capital (G) as cumulative R&D and organizational capital (S) as the cumulative capitalized SG&A for each firm (i) in each year (t) from 1997 to 2021 using the following perpetual inventory model forGandS, respectively:15 The OBS intangible capital is the sum ofGandS. Implicitly, we assume that 100% of R&D expenditures (i.e.,𝛾𝐺γG= 1) and a portion of SG&A (𝛾𝑆γS) should be treated as investment and capitalized. We also assume a 20% depreciation rate for organizational capital (i.e.,𝛿𝑆δS= 0.2). These assumptions are consistent with the literature on estimating intangible capital and help reduce the parameters to be estimated. As discussed inEwens, Peters, and Wang (2024EPW), the relative stability of R&D and SG&A within firms over time, along with the multiplicative functional relationship between the parameters, means that we cannot separately identify the capitalization rate and depreciation rate (𝛾γand𝛿δ) in each capital accumulation process. To estimate the depreciation rate for knowledge capital,𝛿𝐺δG, and the capitalization rate for SG&A expenses,𝛾𝑆γS, we follow the method based on market price of intangible assets as detailed inEwens et al. (2024). We briefly summarize the procedure inAppendix B. To derive the market price of intangible assets, EPW uses two methods. One is based on the acquisition/liquidation/bankruptcy deals from 1996 to 2017 for a selected sample of public firms (labeled as the exit price sample). Intangible assets are valued by the market in these events. This sample begins in 1996 because financial statements from the Securities and Exchange Commission’s (SEC) EDGAR (the Electronic Data Gathering, Analysis, and Retrieval) website are required. The other is based on the trading price of all public firms in the Center for Research in Security Prices (CRSP)/Compustat universe (labeled as the trading price sample). As discussed in EPW, the exit price sample is limited in both the time series and the cross-section. Furthermore, it is subject to concerns such as noisy estimates of goodwill and bankruptcy recovery rates. However, it may provide a more accurate measure of market valuation of intangible assets. In contrast, the trading price sample is much broader and does not have a sample selection bias. It is representative of the population of public firms, although it requires markup estimation for physical assets. More importantly, this method allows us to estimate the capital accumulation parameters using data from 1974 to 1997 so that we can avoid look-ahead bias in computing intangible-adjusted factor returns and examine the impact of intangible adjustment on major factor models from 1998 to 2022. (We start the sample from 1974 because the quality of R&D data is very low before then.) Therefore, we focus on the trading price method and provide more details on how to derive the market price of intangible assets from the trading price inAppendix B. As mentioned, we require no look-ahead bias in computing factor returns. Therefore, we use the trading price sample from 1974 to 1997 to estimate the capital accumulation parameters. We then compute intangible capital and construct the intangible-adjusted factors out of sample (1998–2022). This gives us a set of look-ahead, bias-free, intangible-adjusted factors spanning the period from 1998 to 2022. InTable 1, we report the estimated intangible capital accumulation parameters (capitalization rate for SG&A expenses,𝛾𝑆γS, and depreciation rate for knowledge capital,𝛿𝐺δG) in panel A inTable 1and the summary statistics of knowledge capital, organizational capital, OBS intangibles (the sum ofGandS), and OBS intangible intensity (OBS intangibles scaled by the sum of OBS intangibles and gross PPE) in panel B inTable 1for the Fama–French five industries: Consumer, Manufacturing, High Tech, Health, and Other. As expected, there is significant variation in the intangible capital accumulation parameters across industries. For example, our estimates of the capitalization rate for SG&A expenses,𝛾𝑆γS, range from 0.28 for the Consumer industry to 0.71 for the Other industry. In particular,𝛾𝑆γSvalues are 0.48 and 0.45 for the High Tech industry and the Health industry, respectively. These parameters differ significantly from the 30% capitalization rate typically used in the existing literature and highlight the importance of allowing for industry-specific parameters. Similarly, the depreciation rate for knowledge capital also varies significantly across industries, ranging from 0.11 for Health to 0.36 for Manufacturing. Consistent with the literature, panel B inTable 1shows that the High Tech and Health industries have the highest intangible intensities among the five industries: 0.73 and 0.84, respectively. Manufacturing has the lowest intensity at 0.28. The high intangible intensity along with the heterogeneity across industries highlights the importance of incorporating intangibles into the key characteristics used in forming the factors in prominent factor models. Table 1.Summary Statistics of Intangible Capital Table 1.Summary Statistics of Intangible Capital Notes. The table presents summary statistics for firm intangible capital. Panel A presents the capitalization and depreciation parameters for intangible capital.𝛾𝑆γSrefers to the proportion of SG&A that is capitalized to form organizational capital (S).𝛿𝐺δGdenotes the depreciation rate of knowledge capital (G), which is based on cumulative R&D. Panel B presents means and standard deviations of the stock values for knowledge and organizational capital as well as intangible capital intensity defined as the sum ofSandGscaled by the sum of gross PP&E,S, andG. Each panel consists of five columns corresponding to the Fama–French five industries. The sample period is from 1997 to 2021.
article.4. Intangible Adjustments to Key Charact: In this section, we detail the intangible-adjusted measures of book-to-market ratio, investment, and profitability and the construction of the corresponding factors used in subsequent tests. 4.1. Value Factors As clearly shown in our models, aggregating on- and off-balance sheet book equity can disguise the difference in relationships between expected returns and the two types of assets: tangible and intangible. As shown inEwens et al. (2024), there is a steady increase in the proportion of firm assets composed of intangible capital across all industries in recent decades. This secular trend highlights the need for a larger distinction in this return relationship. Aggregating the two types of capital into a single measure (e.g.,Park 2019,Lev and Srivastava 2022) may obscure such differences. Hence, in addition to traditional B/M, we construct a new B/M measure based on OBS intangibles alone (BOBS/M). Specifically, we first construct the OBS book-to-market ratio as As a comparison with the literature (e.g.,Park 2019), we also construct a “total” measure of the book-to-market equity ratio, which aggregates OBS intangible assets with book equity: 4.2. Investment and Profitability Factors To address the distortion in the investment and profitability factors caused by fully expensing intangible investments under current GAAP, we construct alternative investment and profitability factors. The CMA factor in the Fama–French five-factor model is based on asset growth (AG) as measured by the growth rate in total assets. Our first alternative investment measure, CMAPPE, is constructed based on the growth rate of firms’ stock of PP&E. CMAOBSis constructed based on the growth rate of firms’ OBS intangible assets. The construction method of these alternative investment factors is identical to CMA except for the measure of investment. For example, CMAPPEis based on independent double sorts on size and growth rate of PP&E using New York Stock Exchange (NYSE) breakpoints each year. To illustrate the importance of treating tangible and intangible investments separately, we also construct a total investment factor, CMAtotal, based on growth in combined investment capital (PPE plus OBS intangibles) and use it in the model comparison tests. Hou et al. (2015)construct their investment factor (qINV) based on triple sorts on size, profitability, and investment, where profitability is measured by quarterly ROE and investment is measured by annual asset growth. We use the same procedure in constructing the corresponding intangible investment factor, qINVOBS, based on triple sorts on size, ROEtotalas defined, and growth in OBS intangibles. To capture the capital investment factor, we construct qINVPPEusing triple sorts on size, ROEtotal, and growth in PP&E. Similarly, to illustrate the importance of treating tangible and intangible investments separately, we also construct a total investment factor, qINVtotal, that is based on growth in combined investment capital (PPE plus OBS intangibles). The profitability factor (RMW) in FF5f is based on independent double sorts (2 × 3) on size and OP, where OP is defined as revenues minus cost of goods sold minus SG&A minus R&D expenses minus interest expense all scaled by book equity. To incorporate intangible investments into OP so that the profits are before new investments as indicated in the theoretical frameworks, we adjust operating profits by adding back R&D expenditure and an industry-specific portion of SG&A that should be treated as investment. We also replace the denominator of OP, book equity, with the sum of book equity and OBS to obtain the intangible-adjusted operating profitability, OPtotal: Hou et al. (2015)construct their profitability factor (qROE) based on ROE (net income/book equity). Again, both the numerator and the denominator are affected by accounting rules mandating full expensing of intangible investments. FollowingLev and Sougiannis (1996), we adjust earnings by adding R&D expenditures and a portion of SG&A (net of depreciation of OBS intangible capital).17We also add OBS to BE in the denominator of ROE to obtain intangible-adjusted ROE, ROEtotal: 4.3. Data and Summary of Key Characteristics We obtain accounting information from the Compustat annual and quarterly fundamental files and monthly stock returns from the CRSP. We only keep primary shares (share code of 10, 11). The sample for the returns data is from July 1998 to June 2022. We start the sample in 1998 to avoid look-ahead bias as explained earlier.Table 2presents summary statistics of and Spearman rank correlations between the key characteristics used in constructing the factors for subsequent tests. The correlations between the traditional measures and the corresponding total measures are generally high. Specifically, the correlation between B/M and Btotal/M is 0.79, the correlation between OP and OPtotalis 0.73, and the correlation between ROE and ROEtotalis 0.75. However, the measures based on OBS intangibles tend to have lower correlations with the traditional measures. For example, the correlation between B/M and BOBS/M is 0.54. The correlation between OBSG and PPEG is 0.55. Table 2.Summary Statistics of Key Characteristics Table 2.Summary Statistics of Key Characteristics Notes. The table presents summary statistics for and correlation between firm characteristics. We present the market equity in millions (ME), traditional book-to-market equity (B/M), operating profitability (OP), growth in PP&E (PPEG), and return on equity (ROE). In addition, we present transformations of certain characteristics that adjust for off-balance sheet intangible assets. We use two alternative measures of assets: off-balance sheet assets denoted with the superscriptOBSand sum of on- and off-balance sheet assets denoted by the superscripttotal. OBSG denotes the growth in off-balance sheet intangibles. The variables OP, PPEG, OBSG, and ROE are expressed in percentages. The detailed definitions of these characteristics are explained inAppendix B. The sample period is July 1998 to June 2022.
article.5. Impact of Intangible Adjustments: In this section, we examine the impact of the intangible adjustments on the return predictability of these key characteristics, factor premiums, and the explanatory power of several prominent asset pricing models. 5.1. Impact on Return Predictability of Characteristics A key premise of our extended models is that the relation of returns to tangible and intangible investments is distinct because the characteristics of physical and intangible capital are different. Because our paper focuses on the impact on empirical factor models, we use portfolio sorts to examine the contrast in return predictability between traditional measures of these key characteristics and their intangible-adjusted counterparts. InTable 3, we sort firms into deciles based on these characteristics and report their value- and equal-weighted returns. The portfolios (except for the one based on ROE) are formed at the end of each June, and they are held over the following 12 months. The sample period for the portfolio returns is also from July 1998 to June 2022. Table 3.Decile Portfolios Table 3.Decile Portfolios Notes. This table provides the percentage returns of decile portfolios constructed on the basis of book-to-market (B/M), growth in PP&E (PPEG), return on equity (ROE), and operating profitability (OP). For each measure, we present additional alternatives that account for off-balance sheet intangibles. The variables denoted with the superscriptOBSuse only off-balance sheet intangibles, whereas the variables with the superscripttotaluse both on- and off-balance sheet assets. Panel A (panel B) presents the average returns of the 10 value-weighted (equally weighted) decile portfolios as well as the average return of the high minus low (High − low) portfolio. The sample period is July 1998 to June 2022. Thet-statistics are in parentheses. The average value-weighted high minus low (hedge) portfolio return for B/M sorts is −2 basis points per month, which is statistically insignificant. In comparison, the monthly return for the BOBS/M hedge portfolio is much larger at 46 basis points, although statistically insignificant. The PPEG and OBSG hedge portfolios have negative and statistically insignificant returns. For profitability measures, intangible-adjusted measures generate a significantly larger return spread than traditional ones. Furthermore, the returns spread is statistically significant only for the intangible-adjusted measures. Specifically, the monthly returns of the ROE, ROEtotal, OP, and OPtotalhedge portfolios are 50 (t= 1.27), 73 (t= 2.17), 44 (t= 0.95), and 99 (t= 2.62) basis points, respectively. The pattern is similar and generally stronger with equally weighted returns. 5.2. Impact on Factor Premium Table 4presents summary statistics and Sharpe ratios of the traditional and intangible-adjusted factors for the Fama–French factors (Table 4, panel A) and theq-factors (Table 4, panel B). Among the three versions of value factors (HML, HMLOBS, HMLtotal) inTable 4, panels A and C, HMLOBSgenerates the highest average return and the highest SR. It is also the only value factor that has a statistically significant premium. Specifically, the average returns (t-statistic and SR) are 0.07% (t= 0.34, SR = 0.02), 0.49% (t= 3.42, SR = 0.20), and 0.25% (t= 1.36, SR = 0.08) for HML, HMLOBS, and HMLtotal, respectively. In other words, the factor premium of HMLOBSis seven times as high as that of HML and almost twice as high as that of HMLtotal. The SR of HMLOBSis 10 times as high as that of HML and more than twice as high as that of HMLtotal. These sharp contrasts are consistent with our hypothesis that it is important to consider intangibles and to distinguish between on- and off-balance sheet value measures. To see the differences over time, we plot the cumulative performance of these factors over the years in panel (a) ofFigure 2. The figure shows that the cumulative performance of HMLOBSdominates that of the other two factors almost every year, especially over recent decades. HML peaked at the end of 2006 and has been decreasing since then. Although both HMLtotaland HMLOBSoutperform HML, HMLOBSis much less volatile than HMLtotal. Furthermore, HMLtotalexhibits a significant drawdown after 2016. In sharp contrast, HMLOBShas been steadily climbing during the whole sample period. This contrast over time further illustrates the difference in asset pricing implications between tangible and intangible assets and the necessity of treating them separately. Table 4.Summary Statistics of Factors Table 4.Summary Statistics of Factors Notes. The table presents summary statistics for factors. The first set of factors uses replications of theFama and French (2015)factors (MKT, SMB, HML, CMA, and RMW), and the second set uses replications of theHou et al. (2015)factors (qMKT, qSMB, qINV, and qROE). Each set also shows modified versions of the factors. For the factors that use transformations of the book values of assets, we present modifications that adjust the book values to include off-balance sheet intangible assets. We use two alternative measures of assets: only off-balance sheet assets denoted by the superscriptOBSand both on- and off-balance sheet assets denoted by the superscripttotal. For investment-based factors, we replace asset growth with the growth in PP&E as the sorting characteristic. The resulting factors are indicated with the superscriptPPE. The sample period is from July 1998 to June 2022. The impact on the investment factor is also large. The average return is negative for CMAPPEbut positive for CMAOBS, although both are insignificant. The SRs are 0.01 and −0.02 for CMAOBSand CMAPPE, respectively. The average return of CMAPPEis only −0.05% (t= 0.32). Panel (b) ofFigure 2plots the cumulative performance of CMAPPEand CMAOBSover time. The chart shows that CMAOBSconsistently outperforms CMAPPE, especially over the recent decade. Finally, the impact on the profitability factor, RMW, is also substantial.Table 4shows that intangibles-adjusted profitability factor (RMWtotal) has substantially higher average return and SR than the traditional profitability factor. Specifically, the average returns (t-statistic and SR) are 0.39% (t= 2.14, SR = 0.13) and 0.45% (t= 3.19, SR = 0.19) for RMW and RMWtotal, respectively. In terms of percentage change, the increases in average return and SR are 15.4% and 46.2%, respectively. We plot the cumulative performance of these two versions of profitability factor in panel (c) ofFigure 2. It shows that RMWtotalconsistently outperforms RMW, and the outperformance gets bigger in recent years. In panel B ofTable 4, we show the effect of intangibles on the factors in the HXZ model. For the investment factors, both qINVOBSand qINVPPEhave insignificant average returns. However, qINVOBShas a substantially larger average return and SR than qINVPPE: 0.20% versus 0.08% for average returns and 0.07 versus 0.03 for SR. The intangible adjustment to qROE does not result in a similar improvement as it did for RMW. Incorporating intangibles reduces the average returns (SR) from 0.42% (0.14) for qROE to 0.33% (0.11) for qROEtotal. This difference in the impact may be because RMW is constructed based on annual data, whereas qROE is constructed based on quarterly data. As a majority of quarterly R&D and SG&A are missing from Compustat quarterly, the full effect of intangible adjustments to qROE is hindered by data availability. In panel A ofTable 5, we report the correlations among these factors. The correlation between HML and HMLOBSis low at 0.36. On the other hand, the correlation between RMW and RMWtotalis high at 0.94. We note that the correlations of HMLOBSwith CMAOBS, CMAPPEand RMWtotalare relatively low: 0.17, 0.24, and −0.18, respectively. In contrast, the correlations of HML with CMAPPEand RMW are relatively higher at 0.72 and 0.44, respectively. The pattern is similar for theq-factors in panel B ofTable 5. Table 5.Factor Correlations Table 5.Factor Correlations Notes. The table presents the correlations between some of the factors inTable 4. Panel A uses replications of theFama and French (2015)factors (MKT, SMB, HML, CMA, and RMW) along with modified versions of these factors, and panel B uses replications of theHou et al. (2015)factors (qMKT, qSMB, qINV, and qROE) along with modified versions of these factors. For all factors that use transformations of the book values of assets, we present modifications that adjust the book values to include off-balance sheet intangible assets. We modify the profitability factors(RMW and qROE) to use both on- and off-balance sheet assets to calculate firm-level characteristics and denote the resulting factors with superscripttotal. Factors labeled with the superscriptOBSuse only off-balance sheet intangible assets as the assets of the firms in calculating characteristics. For investment-based factors, we replace asset growth with the growth in PP&E as the sorting characteristic. The resulting factors are indicated with the superscript PPE. The sample period is from July 1998 to June 2022. 5.3. Impact on Asset Pricing Models In this subsection, we test the impact of incorporating intangible investments/capital into well-known factor models on their explanatory power using the Sharpe ratio test developed inBarillas et al. (2020). Specifically, we focus on FF3f first and then, FF5f and HXZ as detailed. As suggested inBarillas and Shanken (2018), to identify the best factor model, comparing their maximum squared Sharpe ratio is a more elegant and powerful way than comparing their ability in explaining a limited number of anomalies. We then rigorously test whether incorporating intangibles significantly improves the performance of these models using the test proposed inBarillas et al. (2020).19For each comparison, we report the optimal weights on the factors in each model, their maximum Sharpe ratios, and thet-statistics of the Sharpe ratio test. When we compare our proposed models with FF5f and HXZ, we first replace their investment factors based on AG with an alternative investment factor based on growth in PP&E as the benchmark models. As suggested byCooper et al. (2024), using asset growth-based investment factors significantly overstates the relative performance of the investment factor in these models. Instead, we use empirical counterparts motivated by our model specification, growth in PP&E, and growth in intangible capital to construct investment factors. As a robustness check, we also compare intangible-adjusted models with the original FF5f and HXZ models by using asset growth to measure investment in the appendix. Both sets of comparisons suggest that incorporating intangibles and treating intangible investment separately from capital investment can improve these models significantly, with substantial weights on intangible-related factors (such as HMLOBS, CMAOBS, and RMWtotal). 5.3.1. Intangibles and the Fama–French Three-Factor Model. InTable 6, we examine the effect of incorporating intangibles into FF3f. The results in panel A ofTable 6show that augmenting FF3f with HMLOBS(Model A) almost doubles its maximum SR from 0.13 to 0.25, although the SR test is statistically insignificant. Furthermore, the optimal weight on HMLOBSis substantial (132%) and dominates the weights on the other three factors. In panel B ofTable 6, we compare the performance of Model A with an alternative FF3f (Model C) in which we replace HML with HMLtotal. The results show that Model C generates a much lower SR of 0.14, although the difference is statistically insignificant. In panel C ofTable 6, we compare Model C with Model B. The results confirm that replacing HML in FF3f with HMLtotaldoes not improve the model’s performance (i.e., it does not have any significant effect on the maximum SR). These comparisons further show that using two separate value factors (HML and HMLOBS) is better at capturing the distinct effects than combining them into one (HMLtotal). Table 6.Intangible Adjustments to the Fama–French Three-Factor Model Table 6.Intangible Adjustments to the Fama–French Three-Factor Model Notes. This table compares models that consider factors constructed using the Fama–French method. In each comparison, the original model is compared with models that incorporate off-balance sheet intangibles. Models A and C refer to the revised models, and Model B refers to the replicated Fama–French model. For each comparison, we report the optimal weights on the factors in the two models being compared, the maximum ex post SRs of the models, and thet-statistics of the Sharpe ratio test based on the difference in squared Sharpe ratios between the two models followingBarillas et al. (2020). HMLOBSis constructed based on off-balance sheet intangible assets (OBS) scaled by market equity. HML is constructed as in the Fama–French three-factor model. HMLtotalis constructed based on the sum of book equity and off-balance sheet intangible assets (OBS) scaled by market equity. The sample period is from July 1998 to June 2022. 5.3.2. Intangibles and the Fama–French Five-Factor and HXZ Models. We next examine the effect of incorporating intangibles into the FF5f and HXZ models with investment factors based on growth in PP&E. We denote these investment factors as CMAPPEand qINVPPE. InTable 7, we focus on FF5f. In panel A ofTable 7, we compare our proposed model (MKT + SMB + HML + HMLOBS+ CMAPPE+ CMAOBS+ RMWtotal) with FF5f (MKT + SMB + HML + CMAPPE+ RMW). The results show that intangible adjustments improve the maximum SR substantially from 0.27 to 0.40. The SR test shows that this difference is statistically significant (t= 2.24). Furthermore, the optimal weights on intangible-adjusted factors in the proposed model are substantial: 0.46, 0.32, and 0.52 for HMLOBS, CMAOBSand RMWtotal, respectively. In contrast, the optimal weights on the traditional factors in the proposed model are much lower: 0.14, 0.05, −0.20, and −0.30 for MKT, SMB, HML, and CMAPPE, respectively. This evidence is consistent with the growing importance of intangible investment as the economy transitioned from manufacturing industries to knowledge- and service-oriented industries. Table 7.Intangible Adjustments to the PPE-Based Fama–French Five-Factor Models Table 7.Intangible Adjustments to the PPE-Based Fama–French Five-Factor Models Notes. This table compares models that consider factors constructed using the Fama–French method based on alternative measures of investment. In each comparison, the change in PP&E (divided by lagged PP&E) is used as an alternative to asset growth in the construction of the investment factor. Similar measures that incorporate off-balance sheet intangibles are used for comparison. Model A refers to the revised model, and Model B refers to the Fama–French model using PP&E for the investment factor CMAPPE. For each comparison, we report the optimal weights on the factors in Models A and B, the maximum ex post SRs of these models, and thet-statistics of the Sharpe ratio test based on the difference in squared Sharpe ratios between Models A and B followingBarillas et al. (2020). HMLOBSis constructed based on off-balance sheet intangible assets (OBS) scaled by market equity. HML is constructed as in the Fama–French three-factor model. CMAPPErefers to the investment factor based on growth in PP&E. Likewise, CMAOBSrefers to the investment factor corresponding to the growth in OBS intangibles. RMWtotalis the profitability factor where profitability is measured relative to total capital. The sample period is from July 1998 to June 2022. In panel B ofTable 7, we show that our proposed model (henceforth, intangible-adjusted FF5f) also significantly outperforms the alternative FF5f with HML replaced by HMLtotal. Thet-statistic for the SR test in this comparison is 2.23. In fact, replacing HML in FF5f with HMLtotalalmost does not affect the maximum SR, which remains at 0.27. As mentioned earlier, it is important to consider the effect of intangibles on all relevant factors. We illustrate this point in panel D ofTable 7. If we only augment FF5f with HMLOBSwithout considering the effect of intangibles on the investment and profitability factors, the improvement in maximum SR is lower but still significant. It increases SR to 0.37, and thet-statistic of the difference in SR relative to FF5f is 2.03. In panel E ofTable 7, we illustrate the importance of using separate HMLs and separate CMAs by comparing our proposed model with FF5f modified with HMLtotal, CMAtotal, and RMWtotal. The results show that our proposed model outperforms at the 10% level with a 25% increase in the Sharpe ratio. We next examine the impact on the HXZ model inTable 8. For the HXZ model, we find that intangible adjustment to the investment and ROE factors increases the model’s SR from 0.27 to 0.30, although the difference is insignificant (see panel A ofTable 8). However, the optimal weight on the intangible investment factor (qINVOBS) dominates that on the tangible investment factor (qINVPPE): 0.55 versus −0.53, respectively. The lack of significance is not surprising, however, as the effect of the adjustment to ROE is limited because of poor coverage of quarterly data for R&D and SG&A in Compustat. This may limit our capability of discovering the true and full impact of intangible adjustment to theq-factor model. Table 8.Intangible Adjustments to the PPE-Basedq-Factor Models Table 8.Intangible Adjustments to the PPE-Basedq-Factor Models Notes. This table compares models that consider factors constructed using theHou et al. (2015)method based on alternative measures of investment. In each comparison, the change in PP&E (divided by lagged PP&E) is used as an alternative to asset growth in the construction of the investment factor. Similar measures that incorporate off-balance sheet intangibles are used for comparison. Model A refers to the revised model, and Model B refers to theq-factor model using PP&E for the investment factor. For each comparison, we report the optimal weights on the factors in Models A and B, the maximum ex post SRs of these models, and thet-statistics of the Sharpe ratio test based on the difference in squared Sharpe ratios between Models A and B followingBarillas et al. (2020). HMLOBSis constructed based on off-balance sheet intangible assets (OBS) scaled by market equity. HML is constructed as in the Fama–French three-factor model. qINVPPErefers to the investment factor based on growth in PP&E. Likewise, qINVOBSrefers to the investment factor corresponding to the growth in OBS intangibles. qROEtotalis the profitability factor where profitability is measured relative to total capital. The sample period is from July 1998 to June 2022. In addition, given the similarity between the FF5f and HXZ models and the important role of HMLOBSobserved in the adjusted FF5f, we also examine its role in the HXZ model by adding HML and HMLOBSto the intangible-adjusted HXZ (see panel B ofTable 8). The results show that adding these additional value factors further improves the Sharpe ratio substantially from 0.27 for HXZ to 0.41. This difference is statistically significant (t= 2.16). Clearly, the driving force of this improvement is HMLOBSwith a substantial weight of 0.47. This pattern is also consistent with what we observe in the adjusted FF5f model earlier. In panel C ofTable 8, we show that our proposed model with separate investment factors also significantly outperforms the intangible-adjustedq-factor model based on total factors at the 5% level. In sum, these tests confirm that including intangibles in the value, investment, and profitability factors can significantly improve the power of both the FF5f model and theq-factor model given that intangibles have become increasingly important over recent decades. Furthermore, it is important to treat tangibles and intangibles separately. 5.3.3. Fama–French Five-Factor Model vs. HXZ Model. In this subsection, we run a horse race between FF5f and the HXZ model in our sample period. In panel A ofTable 9, the SR test shows that the two models have almost identical maximum SR, 0.27, without intangible adjustments. Because the effect of the intangible adjustments to the HXZ model is limited because of the poor coverage of quarterly R&D and SG&A data, we compare the intangible-adjusted FF5f with the (unadjusted) HXZ model in panel B ofTable 9. This comparison can help illustrate the impact of intangible adjustment on theq-factor model indirectly because the two unadjusted models have almost identical SR. The results show that the SR of the intangible-adjusted FF5f exceeds that of the HXZ model by 48% and that this improvement is significant at the 10% level. For completeness, we also compare the intangible-adjusted FF5f with the two versions of the intangible-adjusted HXZ model (with and without the HMLs as shown inTable 9). The results in panel C ofTable 9show that the intangible-adjusted FF5f has a 33% larger maximum SR than the intangible-adjusted HXZ model (without HMLs). When we include the HMLs in the intangible-adjusted HXZ model, the adjusted FF5f model has a similar SR as the intangible-adjusted HXZ model, as shown in panel D ofTable 9. However, the difference in SR between the two adjusted models is statistically insignificant in both sets of comparisons. Table 9.Comparison of the PPE-Based Fama–French Five-Factor andq-Factor Models Table 9.Comparison of the PPE-Based Fama–French Five-Factor andq-Factor Models Notes. This table compares models that consider factors constructed using the Fama–French and Hou et al. (2015) methods based on alternative measures of investment. In each comparison, the change in PP&E (divided by lagged PP&E) is used as an alternative to asset growth in the construction of the investment factor. Similar measures that incorporate off-balance sheet intangibles are used for comparison. The sample period is from July 1998 to June 2022. These comparisons suggest that the intangible-adjusted FF5f is a cleaner model to use in terms of illustrating the impact of incorporating intangibles to factor models because it is not subject to the quarterly data quality issue for the HXZ model. 5.4. Impact on Anomalies In this subsection, we examine the practical implications of these intangible-adjusted models in terms of explaining existing anomalies documented in the literature. We start with the 207 anomalies listed in tables 3 and 4 ofChen and Zimmermann (2022). We then only keep those 81 anomalies with a significant raw return for the long-short hedge portfolio at the 5% level in our sample period. To check the performance of different models in explaining these anomalies, we run 81 time-series regressions of the hedge portfolios’ monthly returns on the following empirical factor model specifications: FF5f, intangible-adjusted FF5f (FF5f + OBS), HXZ, and the intangible-adjusted HXZ model with HML and HMLOBS(HXZ + OBS).Table 10reports the summary of these regressions for the 81 anomalies. Specifically, we report the average and median magnitudes of the 81 alphas (intercepts from the time-series regressions), the number of times a factor model generates the highest adjustedR2among the four models (i.e., best-fit model), and the number (and percentage) of alphas significant at the 1% level. Table 10.Comparison of Factor Models in Explaining Anomalies Table 10.Comparison of Factor Models in Explaining Anomalies Notes. This table compares the performance of traditional factor models as well as intangible-adjusted factor models in explaining documented anomalies. The 81 anomaly returns are obtained fromChen and Zimmermann (2022)based on the criteria that their long-short portfolio’s return is significant at the 5% level over the sample period from July 1998 to June 2022. For each anomaly, we estimate its long-short portfolio’s alpha from four different factor models: FF5f, intangible-adjusted FF5f (FF5f + OBS), HXZ (HXZ), and intangible-adjusted HXZ (HXZ + OBS). We report the average and median magnitude of the 81 alphas (intercepts from the time-series factor regressions), the number of times a factor model generates the highest adjustedR2among the four models (i.e., best-fit model), and the number (and percentage) of alphas that remain significant at the 1% level. Consistent with the SR tests, all of these aspects suggest a significant improvement from intangible adjustments. For example, the average magnitude of these monthly alphas is reduced from 0.70% for FF5f to 0.60% for intangible-adjusted FF5f (a 14.29% reduction) and from 0.68% for HXZ to 0.62% for HXZ + OBS (a 9% reduction). The improvement is even larger for median magnitude of these alphas (a 17% reduction and a 13.6% reduction, respectively). Furthermore, the contrast in the number of times a model is ranked as the best-fit model (based on adjustedR2) is very sharp: 3, 29, 9, and 40 for FF5f, FF5f + OBS, HXZ, and HXZ + OBS, respectively. The contrast in the percentage of anomalies that cannot be fully explained by a model is also big. We find that FF5f cannot explain 72% of these 81 anomalies at the 1% level. In contrast, FF5f + OBS cannot fully explain 57% of these anomalies. Similarly, HXZ cannot explain 68% of these anomalies, whereas HXZ + OBS cannot explain 60% of them. Overall, these results illustrate the economic significance and practical implications of intangible adjustments to the prominent factor models.
article.6. Robustness: In this section, we conduct several robustness checks and report most of the results in the online appendix. First, we conduct the key SR tests using the investment factor based on AG (instead of PPEG) as inFama and French (2015)andHou et al. (2015)to be more comparable with their models. We then test the effectiveness of these AG-based factors in explaining anomalies. Additionally, we conduct the SR tests over the extended sample (July 1977 to June 2022) and the early sample period (July 1977 to June 1998) to check the time variation in the impact of incorporating intangibles on asset pricing models. Lastly, we conduct the SR tests using factors based on industry-demeaned characteristics to check whether our results are robust to industry adjustment.20 Table 11shows that the results are, in general, robust to using asset growth (instead of PPEG) to construct the investment factor. The intangible adjustment yields a substantial and significant improvement over the original FF5f. The SR increases from 0.34 to 0.43, a 26% increase. Although the intangible adjustment results in insignificant improvement over the originalq-factor model, the SR increases from 0.34 to 0.41, a 21% increase.21In the Online Appendix, we repeat the analysis ofTable 10but use the AG-based factors. Table IA.6 in the Online Appendix shows that the intangible-adjusted FF5f andq-factor models continue to explain more anomalies than the original models. Table 11.Intangible Adjustments to Asset Growth-Based Factor Models Table 11.Intangible Adjustments to Asset Growth-Based Factor Models Notes. This table compares models that consider factors constructed using the Fama–French and Hou et al. (2015) methods. In each model, asset growth is used as the proxy for investment in the factor construction. The augmented models use measures that incorporate off-balance sheet intangibles. For each comparison, we report the optimal weights on the factors in Models A and B, the maximum ex post SRs of these models, and thet-statistics of the Sharpe ratio test based on the difference in squared Sharpe ratios between Models A and B followingBarillas et al. (2020). The sample period is from July 1998 to June 2022. To show the time variation in the impact of intangibles, we extend the sample backward to include the intangible accumulation parameter estimation period and report the model comparison (Sharpe ratio) tests for the extended full sample (July 1977 to June 2022) as well as the early period (July 1977 to June 1998) in the Online Appendix (see Tables IA.1–IA.4). The results in the extended full sample confirm what we observe in the later period (Tables 7–10in the main tests), and the results in the early sample period, in general, show a weaker improvement from intangibles compared with the results in the later sample period. The out-of-sample tests (later period) are cleaner and subject to higher hurdles relative to the in-sample tests (early period). However, the two sets of results still show that the improvement from incorporating OBS intangibles is larger over recent decades. This contrast is consistent with the tremendous growth in OBS intangibles over recent decades. Finally, in the Online Appendix, we create factors based on the Fama–French 12 industry-demeaned characteristics and conduct the key Sharpe ratio tests for model comparisons using these factors. We show that the improvements from intangible adjustments over existing factor models are still substantial and significant. Therefore, our results are robust to using these factors based on industry-demeaned characteristics.
article.7. Conclusion: We examine the asset pricing implication of incorporating off-balance sheet intangible assets to address the potentially distorting effects of full expensing of R&D and SG&A on value, profitability, and investment. We show both conceptually and empirically that considering intangible investment/assets in popular empirical factor models significantly increases their performance in explaining test asset returns. This is especially important over recent decades in which intangible capital has become increasingly crucial to firm value. Our evidence suggests that the relative importance of the value factor in popular empirical factor models is grossly understated when intangibles are not considered. We find that the premium associated with an OBS intangibles-based value factor, HMLOBS, is seven times as high as the original value premium proposed byFama and French (1993)in our sample period. Including HMLOBSsignificantly improves the relative performance of the original three-factor model ofFama and French (1993)compared with the newly proposed Fama–French five-factor andq-factor models. Furthermore, we show that once intangible capital/investment is accounted for, the value factors are no longer redundant in eitherFama and French (2015)orHou et al. (2015). Incorporating intangible investments into the operating profitability factor in FF5f (RMW) also results in a substantial increase in both premium and Sharpe ratio compared with the original RMW factor. More importantly, we show that inBarillas et al. (2020)model comparison tests, intangible adjustments to the value, investment, and profitability factors together can significantly improve the performance of these factor models, confirming the importance of systematically accounting for intangibles in this setting. These findings have a significant and far-reaching impact on many aspects of investment, such as factor investing strategies, fund performance evaluation, cost of capital calculations, and capital budgeting.

references: Amenc N, Goltz F, Luyten B ( 2020 ) Intangible capital and the value factor: Has your value definition just expired? J. Portfolio Management 46:83–99.
